section.title: The Coming King
section.sectionRationale: The first of the two closing burdens opens with judgment sweeping down the coastlands, then lands on the book's most famous royal promise: Rejoice, your King comes to you, righteous and victorious, humble and riding on a donkey. Jesus fulfilled this word exactly on Palm Sunday. A conquering God clears the way for a humble, peace-proclaiming King whose dominion reaches to the ends of the earth.
section.thesis: The LORD who marches in judgment against the proud nations comes to save His people through a humble King riding on a donkey, who proclaims peace to the nations, frees prisoners by the blood of the covenant, and reigns from sea to sea.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the burden of judgment against Israel's northern and Philistine neighbors, ending with God camping to guard His house; the coming of Zion's humble King who proclaims peace and rules to the ends of the earth; and the LORD appearing to save His people, prisoners of hope restored double.

verses.text: This is the {{massa|burden}} of the word of the LORD against the land of Hadrach, and Damascus its resting place, for the eyes of men and of all the tribes of Israel are upon the LORD,
and also against Hamath, which borders it, as well as Tyre and Sidon, though they are very shrewd.
Tyre has built herself a fortress; she has heaped up silver like dust, and gold like the dirt of the streets.
Behold, the Lord will impoverish her and cast her wealth into the sea, and she will be consumed by fire.
Ashkelon will see and fear; Gaza will writhe in agony, as will Ekron, for her hope will wither. There will cease to be a king in Gaza, and Ashkelon will be uninhabited.
A mixed race will occupy Ashdod, and I will cut off the pride of the Philistines.

I will remove the blood from their mouths and the abominations from between their teeth. Then they too will become a remnant for our God; they will become like a clan in Judah, and Ekron will be like the Jebusites.
But I will camp around My house because of an army, because of those who march to and fro, and never again will an oppressor overrun My people, for now I keep watch with My own eyes.

units.label: Your King comes on a donkey

verses.text: Rejoice greatly, O Daughter of Zion! Shout in triumph, O Daughter of Jerusalem! See, your King comes to you, {{tsaddiq|righteous}} and victorious, {{ani|humble}} and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.

And I will cut off the chariot from Ephraim and the horse from Jerusalem, and the bow of war will be broken. Then He will proclaim peace to the nations. His dominion will extend from sea to sea, and from the Euphrates to the ends of the earth.
As for you, because of the blood of My {{berith|covenant}}, I will release your prisoners from the waterless pit.
Return to your stronghold, O prisoners of hope; even today I declare that I will restore to you double.
For I will bend Judah as My bow and fit it with Ephraim. I will rouse your sons, O Zion, against the sons of Greece. I will make you like the sword of a mighty man.

units.label: The LORD will save His people
units.note: the flock like jewels in a crown

verses.text: Then the LORD will appear over them, and His arrow will go forth like lightning. The Lord GOD will sound the ram's horn and advance in the whirlwinds of the south.

The LORD of Hosts will shield them. They will destroy and conquer with slingstones; they will drink and roar as with wine. And they will be filled like sprinkling bowls, drenched like the corners of the altar.
On that day the LORD their God will save them as the flock of His people; for like jewels in a crown they will sparkle over His land.
How lovely they will be, and how beautiful! Grain will make the young men flourish, and new wine, the young women.

massa.gloss: A burden or oracle, a weighty word of the LORD, often one of judgment. The term opens both closing sections of Zechariah, chapters 9 and 12, marking them as heavy pronouncements laid on the prophet. It signals that what follows is a solemn divine verdict, here beginning with judgment on the nations and rising to the promise of the King.

tsaddiq.gloss: Righteous, just, in the right. The coming King is righteous in Himself and brings righteousness to His people, ruling with perfect justice rather than the corruption of earthly kings. The word is paired here with being saved or endowed with salvation, so the King is both morally right and the bringer of rescue, a righteousness that saves.

ani.gloss: Humble, lowly, afflicted, poor. The King comes not in the pomp of a warhorse but ani, lowly, mounted on a donkey, the animal of peace and of a servant. The word marks Him as the gentle, afflicted King foretold to identify with the lowly, fulfilled when Jesus, meek and lowly in heart, rode into Jerusalem on a donkey's colt.

berith.gloss: Covenant, a binding relationship sealed with blood. The blood of My covenant is the sacrificial blood that ratifies God's bond with His people and secures their release from the pit. It looks back to the covenant sealed at Sinai and forward to the blood of the new covenant that Jesus poured out, by which the true prisoners of sin and death are set free.

basic.context: This is the first of two closing burdens. It opens with God's judgment sweeping down through Israel's neighbors, Aram, Phoenicia, and Philistia, while He promises to guard His own house. Then comes the great turn: Zion is told to rejoice because her King is coming, righteous and bringing salvation, yet humble, riding on a donkey. He will end warfare, proclaim peace to the nations, and reign to the ends of the earth. By the blood of the covenant He frees prisoners, and the LORD Himself appears to save His flock, who shine like jewels in a crown.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic poetry combining judgment and salvation. The judgment oracle clears the stage for the King. Verse 9 is directly messianic; the New Testament shows Jesus deliberately fulfilling it on Palm Sunday. The King's peaceful, worldwide reign reaches beyond any ancient ruler to Christ. The military imagery of the last section pictures God's victory for His people, ultimately the spiritual victory won in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The section is a massa, a burden, a weighty oracle. The King is tsaddiq, righteous and saving, yet ani, humble and lowly, riding a donkey rather than a warhorse. And prisoners are freed because of the blood of the berith, the covenant, the sacrificial blood that binds God to His people and secures their release.
basic.typology: The humble King on a donkey is fulfilled exactly in Jesus at the Triumphal Entry. The King who proclaims peace to the nations and reigns from sea to sea is Christ, whose gospel of peace goes to the ends of the earth. The prisoners freed by the blood of the covenant point to those released from sin and death by the blood of Jesus.
basic.christ: Verse 9 is one of the most precisely fulfilled prophecies in the Bible. Matthew and John both cite it as Jesus rides into Jerusalem on a donkey's colt, presenting Himself as the promised King, righteous and bringing salvation, gentle and lowly. He does not come on a warhorse to crush but on a donkey to save, and He proclaims peace to the nations. His blood is the blood of the covenant that frees the true prisoners of hope.
basic.god: God is a warrior who judges the proud and shrewd nations, casting down their wealth and pride, yet He is also the Savior who guards His house and gathers His flock. Most strikingly, He saves not by overwhelming force but through a humble King, revealing that His power reaches its goal in gentleness, peace, and covenant blood.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 21 and John 12 cite Zechariah 9:9 at the Triumphal Entry. Ephesians 2 says Christ came and preached peace to those far and near. Isaiah 9 promises a government of endless peace. Hebrews 13 speaks of the blood of the eternal covenant.
basic.oneStory: God clears the way in judgment and then sends the King the whole story has awaited: humble on a donkey, proclaiming peace, reigning to the ends of the earth, freeing prisoners by covenant blood. This is Jesus on Palm Sunday and beyond, and the story runs on to His worldwide reign of peace and the final gathering of His flock to shine like jewels in His crown.

context.literary: The opening of the first of two burdens, chapters 9 to 11, that close the book with a different style from the dated night visions. The oracle moves from judgment on the nations to the coming King and God's salvation of His flock, setting a pattern of contrast between worldly power and God's humble, saving reign that runs through the rest of the book.
context.historical: The route of judgment in verses 1 to 8 traces the path a conquering army would take from the north down the coast, which many connect with the later campaign of Alexander the Great, who indeed passed by Jerusalem while devastating Tyre and the Philistine cities. Against that backdrop of imperial conquest, God promises a wholly different kind of King for His people.
context.canonical: Zechariah 9:9 becomes one of the clearest messianic texts, cited at the Triumphal Entry in Matthew 21 and John 12. The humble, peace-proclaiming King joins Isaiah's Prince of Peace and Psalm 72's righteous king whose dominion runs from sea to sea, all fulfilled in Christ, whose gospel of peace reaches the ends of the earth.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce that the LORD will judge the proud nations and guard His people, and then to give Zion joyful hope in a coming King who is righteous and saving yet humble, who ends war and proclaims peace to the nations, frees prisoners by covenant blood, and reigns to the ends of the earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The oracle is prophetic promise, describing God's judgment and salvation and the coming King, not issuing commands. The abiding truths are God's rule over the nations, the character of the humble saving King fulfilled in Christ, and freedom secured by covenant blood.

debates.question: How literally should the judgment march of verses 1 to 8 be tied to Alexander the Great?

views.label: A specific foretelling of Alexander's campaign
views.case: The precise route down through Hadrach, Damascus, Tyre, and the Philistine cities matches Alexander's fourth-century advance, and God's promise to guard His house fits Jerusalem being spared.

views.label: A representative picture of God judging the nations
views.case: The oracle need not name one conqueror; it portrays God's sovereign judgment on the proud powers around Israel, whatever historical form it took, to set the stage for the King.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage shows God ruling the nations in judgment and clearing the way for the coming King who saves His people.

debates.question: Is the King's warfare in verses 13 to 15 physical or spiritual?

views.label: God's real defense of His people in history
views.case: The imagery of bow, sword, and slingstones pictures God fighting for His people against their enemies, including struggles like the Maccabean resistance against the sons of Greece.

views.label: A picture of the spiritual victory won in Christ
views.case: In light of the humble King who proclaims peace, the battle language ultimately portrays the greater, spiritual conquest Christ wins for His people over sin, death, and the powers.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that God fights and wins for His people, a victory that culminates in the saving reign of the humble King.

study.originalLanguages: The oracle is introduced as a massa, a burden, a heavy word of the LORD that begins in judgment and rises to salvation. Its center is the King who is tsaddiq, righteous and just, and at the same time endowed with salvation, so that His righteousness is a rescuing righteousness; yet He is ani, humble, lowly, afflicted, coming not on the warhorse of a conqueror but on a donkey, the mount of peace and of a servant. And the ground of the prisoners' release is the blood of the berith, the covenant, the sacrificial blood that binds God to His people, looking back to Sinai and forward to the cup Jesus called the blood of the new covenant poured out for many. Righteous and saving, humble and gentle, sealing freedom with covenant blood, the King of Zechariah 9 is unmistakably the Lord Jesus.

typology.type: The humble King riding on a donkey
typology.fulfillment: Jesus at the Triumphal Entry, presenting Himself as Zion's promised King, Matthew 21:4-5 and John 12:14-15.

typology.type: The King who proclaims peace to the nations
typology.fulfillment: Christ who came and preached peace to those far and near, reigning to the ends of the earth, Ephesians 2:17 and Psalm 72:8.

typology.type: Prisoners freed by the blood of the covenant
typology.fulfillment: Those released from sin and death by the blood of the new covenant in Christ, Hebrews 13:20 and Matthew 26:28.

study.christ: This chapter contains one of Scripture's most exactly fulfilled prophecies. Zion is told to shout for joy because her King is coming, and He comes not as earthly kings do. He is righteous and endowed with salvation, but He is lowly, riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey. Centuries later Jesus deliberately arranged to enter Jerusalem in precisely this way, and both Matthew and John tell us it happened to fulfill this very word. He refused the warhorse and chose the donkey, coming in peace to save rather than in force to crush, gentle and lowly in heart. He cuts off the chariot and the battle bow and proclaims peace to the nations, and His dominion, unlike any Persian or Greek empire, runs from sea to sea to the ends of the earth as the gospel of peace spreads. And the release of the prisoners of hope from the waterless pit, because of the blood of My covenant, is accomplished when Jesus pours out His blood, the blood of the new covenant, to set the captives of sin and death eternally free.
study.god: The God of this oracle is at once a mighty warrior and a gentle Savior. He marches against the proud and shrewd nations, humbling Tyre's boasted wealth and cutting off Philistine pride, and He camps as a guard around His own house so no oppressor will overrun His people. Yet the deepest revelation of His power is the kind of King He sends: not a conqueror on a warhorse but a humble King on a donkey, who ends warfare and proclaims peace. Here we learn that God's strength reaches its purpose in gentleness, that He saves by covenant blood rather than by brute force, and that His aim is a worldwide reign of peace in which His rescued flock sparkle like jewels in a crown. The warrior and the peace-bringer are the same faithful God.

crossReferences.note: The Triumphal Entry is said to fulfill Zechariah 9:9, your King comes to you, humble and mounted on a donkey.
John cites the same prophecy as Jesus rides into Jerusalem on a donkey's colt.
Christ came and preached peace to those far off and those near, fulfilling the King who proclaims peace to the nations.
This is My blood of the covenant, poured out for many, the covenant blood by which prisoners are freed.

study.oneStory: God clears the way in judgment and sends the King the story has long awaited: righteous and saving, humble on a donkey, proclaiming peace and reigning to the ends of the earth, freeing prisoners by covenant blood. This is Jesus on Palm Sunday and in His worldwide gospel, and the story presses on to His reign of peace and the gathering of His flock to shine like jewels in the crown of their King.

quiz.q: How does Zion's King come, according to verse 9?

options.text: Righteous and saving, yet humble, riding on a donkey.
options.note: The King is righteous and endowed with salvation, humble on a donkey's colt.

options.text: Proud and armed, riding a warhorse into battle.
options.note: The opposite: He comes lowly on a donkey, not a warhorse.

options.text: In secret, unnoticed by anyone.
options.note: Zion is told to rejoice greatly and shout, a public coming.

options.text: As a foreign conqueror to enslave the people.
options.note: He comes to save His people, not to enslave them.

quiz.q: How was this prophecy fulfilled in Jesus?

options.text: He rode into Jerusalem on a donkey's colt at the Triumphal Entry.
options.note: Matthew 21 and John 12 cite this very prophecy.

options.text: He led an army against Rome.
options.note: He came in peace on a donkey, not leading an army.

options.text: He never entered Jerusalem.
options.note: He entered publicly, fulfilling the prophecy exactly.

options.text: He rejected the title of King entirely.
options.note: He presented Himself as the promised King fulfilling this word.

quiz.q: What is the scope of the King's dominion?

options.text: From sea to sea, to the ends of the earth, proclaiming peace to the nations.
options.note: Verse 10 describes a worldwide reign of peace.

options.text: Only the small territory of Judah.
options.note: His dominion reaches far beyond Judah to the ends of the earth.

options.text: Only the city of Jerusalem.
options.note: His rule extends worldwide, not to one city.

options.text: No dominion at all.
options.note: His dominion extends from sea to sea.

quiz.q: On what basis are the prisoners of hope released?

options.text: Because of the blood of God's covenant.
options.note: Verse 11, because of the blood of My covenant, I will release your prisoners.

options.text: Because they paid their own ransom.
options.note: Release comes by covenant blood, not self-payment.

options.text: Because the nations set them free.
options.note: God frees them by covenant blood, not the nations.

options.text: They are never released.
options.note: God promises to release them and restore double.

quiz.q: What does the humble King reveal about God's power?

options.text: God's strength reaches its purpose in gentleness, peace, and covenant blood.
options.note: God saves through a humble King, not brute force.

options.text: God can only act through overwhelming violence.
options.note: He saves through humility and peace, not only force.

options.text: God is weak and unable to judge the nations.
options.note: He judges the nations mightily, yet saves through humility.

options.text: God has no interest in the nations.
options.note: The King proclaims peace to the nations and rules to the ends of the earth.

questions: Your King came humble on a donkey, not proud on a warhorse. Where do you look for strength in power and status rather than in the way of humble Christ?
The King proclaims peace to the nations. How does knowing His reign reaches the ends of the earth shape your prayers and hopes for the world?
Prisoners of hope are freed by the blood of the covenant. What captivity in your life do you need to bring to the freeing blood of Jesus?
God saves His flock so they shine like jewels in a crown. How does it change your self-view to know God treasures His people as His own jewels?
Zion was told to rejoice greatly at the King's coming. What would it look like for real joy in Christ the King to mark your life this week?

translationNotes.note: Victorious can also be rendered endowed with salvation; the King is both righteous and the bringer of rescue. The verse is cited in Matthew 21:5 and John 12:15.
Ephraim names the northern kingdom of Israel; from the Euphrates renders the Hebrew the River, marking the sweep of the King's dominion to the ends of the earth.
The sons of Greece renders the Hebrew the sons of Javan, often connected with later conflict against the Greek empire, showing God rousing His people against a coming world power.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 9:1-17. All 17 verses reproduced in order. Source em dashes in verses 1, 4, 5, 7 (poetic line breaks) rendered as commas and clean breaks per style.
